Minds that made money · 14
He bought when there was blood in the streets.
His biggest buys came in wartime.
1939. Hitler had just invaded Poland. He borrowed money to buy stocks nobody wanted.
Meet the man who bought the panic →
02
Who
John
Templeton
1912–2008. Pioneer of global value investing — he ran his money from the Bahamas and bought where everyone else was running.
Only 4 of 104
stocks he bought in 1939 ever went to zero.
Born Nov 29, 1912, Winchester, TN; died Jul 8, 2008, Nassau, Bahamas. Renounced US citizenship 1964. Sources: Wikipedia; Motley Fool; LGT.
03
104 stocks.
Only 4 went to zero.
104 NYSE stocks bought · 1939
~4×
in about 4 years
~$10,000 borrowed → roughly quadrupled
He bought $100 of every NYSE stock under $1 — then held through a world war.
104 companies bought at/under $1, 100 shares each; only 4 became worthless (Wikipedia, Motley Fool, LGT). ~$10k borrowed and ~4× are approximate, illustrative round numbers (~$100 × 104 ≈ $10,400). Held ~4 years. Sources: Motley Fool; LGT; Wikipedia.
04
“
The time of maximum pessimism is the best time to buy.
John TempletonThe time of maximum optimism is the best time to sell.
What most people miss →
05
It wasn't one brave bet.
It was 104 small ones — because he knew most would fail.
Dozens were already bankrupt. He spread tiny stakes across survival odds, not conviction. "Buy the blood" without the basket is just catching a falling knife.
06
One question
When the headlines are scariest, can you stay in your seat?
Most can buy the dip. Almost nobody can hold it for four years while everyone tells them they're wrong.
Save this for the next time the headlines try to shake you out.
07
He didn't time the bottom.
He bought the fear.
Now you've got
AI on your side.
Save it · Share it · Follow @hellojarvis_us for more
Education only. Not investment advice. Figures rounded and illustrative. Sources: Wikipedia; Motley Fool; LGT; Franklin Templeton "Templeton Maxims"; Washington Post (2008 obit).

Caption — Instagram post body

In 1939, the world was ending. Hitler had just rolled into Poland, the market was in free-fall, and a 26-year-old phoned his broker with one order: buy $100 of every stock on the NYSE trading under a dollar. He borrowed the money to do it.

Most of those companies were garbage. Dozens were already bankrupt. John Templeton knew that — that was the point. He didn't bet on one comeback story. He bought 104 of them, tiny stakes spread across the wreckage, because he was betting on survival odds, not a hunch. Of the 104, only 4 ever went to zero. He held the rest for about four years, through a world war, and roughly quadrupled his money.

Here's the part everyone skips. The famous "buy when there's blood in the streets" line wasn't even his — it's Baron Rothschild's. And the genius wasn't the nerve to buy. Nerve is cheap. The genius was the basket and the four years of sitting on junk while every headline screamed you were wrong.

So when the next crash comes and your feed is on fire: the buying isn't the hard part. The holding is. Markets are scary on purpose — fear sells clicks. Your plan doesn't need you to be brave. It needs you to stay in your seat.

He didn't time the bottom. He out-sat it.

Save this for the next time the headlines try to shake you out. Send it to the friend who panic-sold.

Education only. Not investment advice. Sources: Wikipedia · Motley Fool · LGT · Franklin Templeton "Templeton Maxims" · Washington Post (2008)
